mnemonics.title: Critical Section Requirements
mnemonics.content: MPB - Mutual exclusion, Progress, Bounded waiting - A solution must have all three!

mnemonics.title: Peterson's Solution Variables
mnemonics.content: TWO flags and ONE turn: 'flag' shows interest, 'turn' decides who goes

mnemonics.title: Semaphore Operations
mnemonics.content: W AIT (decrements, may block), S IGNAL (increments, may wake)

mnemonics.title: Test-and-Set Behavior
mnemonics.content: TAS: Test old value, ATOMICALLY Set to true - always returns old value

acronyms.usage: Code segment accessing shared resources requiring mutual exclusion
Hardware atomic instruction for mutual exclusion
Scheduling method not required by critical section problem
Variables in Peterson's solution: flag array and turn variable

visualTips.visualization: Imagine two people at a door. 'flag' is like holding up a 'I want to enter' sign. 'turn' is like a traffic signal deciding who goes when both want to enter.
Think of a toll booth with a counter. wait() is like taking a ticket (decrement), signal() is like returning a ticket (increment). When counter is 0, you wait in line.
Imagine a turnstile that automatically locks after someone walks through - it both checks and sets in one atomic action.
Like a queue at a ticket counter where after you request, only a limited number of people ahead of you can be served before it's your turn.

analogies.analogy: Like a single-occupancy bathroom - only one person can use it at a time to maintain hygiene (data consistency).
Two people trying to update the same Google Doc simultaneously without locking - whoever saves last overwrites the other's changes.
Repeatedly checking if your friend has finished on the phone instead of them calling you back - wastes your time waiting.
A parking lot with limited spaces. The counter shows available spots. When full, cars wait in a queue. When a car leaves, one waiting car enters.
A bank teller with a rope - only one customer can approach the counter at a time, even though multiple tellers (procedures) exist.
